text: The Jesuits, among the most influential missionary organizations in history, maintained a significant presence in the Sonoran Desert—a transborder region now encompassing the Mexican state of Sonora and the U.S. state of Arizona. Following the foundational work of the pioneer Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711), the early eighteenth century saw an influx of German-speaking missionaries who expanded this vast evangelical network. Beyond their proselytizing efforts, these individuals served as meticulous scholars of the frontier, producing an extensive corpus of records documenting their scientific observations, cultural encounters, and daily experiences. Although the Order faced expulsion in 1767 and a global suppression in 1773, their legacy remains preserved in these rich archival materials. Today, these missionary accounts serve as vital historical, anthropological, and scientific primary sources, offering profound insights into the colonial encounter and the development of the North American Borderlands.
